elements.sent-2411-001: We investigated the temporal and spectral features of $\gamma$ Cassiopeiae's X-ray emission within the context of the white dwarf accretion hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-002: We find that the variabilities present in the X-ray data show two different signals, one primarily due to absorption and the other due to flickering like in non-magnetic cataclysmic variables.
elements.sent-2411-003: We then use this two-component insight to investigate previously un-reported simultaneous XMM and NuSTAR data.
elements.sent-2411-004: The model fitting results find white dwarf properties consistent with optical studies alongside a significant secondary, thermal source.
elements.sent-2411-005: We propose a secondary shock between the Be decretion disk and white dwarf accretion disk as the source.
elements.sent-2411-006: Finally, we analyzed a unique, low-count rate event of the XMM light curve as potential evidence for the white dwarf encountering Be decretion disk structures.
elements.sent-2411-007: For the last 30 years, the X-ray emission of $\gamma$ Cassiopeia has been one the most difficult systems to understand.
elements.sent-2411-008: What makes the system so enigmatic is not its uniqueness, given that a number of analogues have been found (e.g., $\pi$\,Aqr \citep{Naze2017,Tsujimoto2023,Huenemoerder2024 , $\zeta$\,Tau \citep{Naze2024 , HD 110432 \citep{Tsujimoto2018 , HD 119682 \citep{Naze2020 , and HD 45314 \citep{Rauw2013 ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The problem arises from the nature of its X-ray spectra compared to other OB stars.
elements.sent-2411-010: In Figure~\ref{fig:3StarCompare we show $\gamma$\,Cas's Chandra High Energy Transmission Grating (HETG) X-ray spectra compared to similar Chandra observations of $\zeta$ Puppis \citep[a ``normal'' OB star;][]{Cohen2014,Huenemoerder2020 and $\theta^1$\,Ori\,C \citep[a strongly magnetic O star;][]{Naze2014 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: What these spectra show is that $\gamma$\,Cas is not only exceptionally bright across all wavelengths but its X-ray source mechanism is much hotter than the others.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{3_stars_ene-01.pdf} \caption{Comparison of the X-ray spectra of three different types of OB stars: $\zeta$ Puppis \citep[embedded wind shocks;][and citations therein]{Cohen2014,Huenemoerder2020}, $\theta^1$\,Ori\,C \citep[magnetically confined wind shocks,][]{Naze2014}, and $\gamma$\,Cas (unknown). All spectra are the Chandra HEG+MEG plotted on the MEG grid. See Tables~\ref{tab:OtherObsIds} and \ref{tbl:oriobs} for information on the $\zeta$\,Pup and $\theta^1$\,Ori\,C datasets used.} \label{fig:3StarCompare}
elements.sent-2411-012: These variabilities occur at all timescales, from short time fluctuations within observations and longer period ``disk eruptions'' \citep{Rauw2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: There are additional sub-variability structures that \citet{Smith2019 refer to as ``softness dips'' that are characterized as short periods of decreased soft counts and increased hardness ratio in the light curves.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The combination of the hot X-ray emission and strong variability is why the X-ray emission mechanism has been difficult to decipher.
elements.sent-2411-015: A number of models have been proposed, though, ranging from complex magnetic interactions to accretion onto a compact object; see \citet{Langer2020 for a detailed review of each of the proposed models.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The magnetic interaction picture, more specifically the coupling between the Be star and its disk, has been the most ubiquitous model argued since its inception.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, \citet{Langer2020 argues that this magnetic picture is unlikely to produce the X-rays and is too difficult, if not impossible, to test.}
elements.sent-2411-018: On the accretion side, work has focused on the possibility of the compact object being a white dwarf \Change{\citep[WD;][]{Murakami1986,Tsujimoto2018,Gies2023 based on the similarities in the spectra between $\gamma$\,Cas stars and cataclysmic variables.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The accretion generated X-rays have a unique signature from their emission measure distribution \citep{Fabian1994 and fluorescent lines like Fe~K~$\alpha$ at 6.4\,keV \citep{Mukai2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Comparatively little work has been done to explain the temporal variations in the context of WD emission.
elements.sent-2411-021: Our goal is to do so to explain the variability in $\gamma$\,Cas.
elements.sent-2411-022: At the same time, we will support this temporal analysis by also looking at the spectral data in specific time slices and at different energy bands to form a complete temporal spectral picture of the system.
elements.sent-2411-023: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-024: In \S~\ref{sec:DataReduction , we give details to the X-ray observations we use and the data reduction methods.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The Observation IDs of all data sets used in this paper are given in Appendix~\ref{sec:ObsIDs .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In \S~\ref{sec:Variability , we analyze the types of variability that $\gamma$\,Cas could be undergoing.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In \S~\ref{sec:SpectralFitting , we fit simultaneous XMM and NuSTAR data and make time-sliced spectra to investigate light curve features.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Finally, in \S~\ref{sec:Conclusions we give our conclusions and proposed future work.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The table of observations used in our analysis are given in Table~\ref{tab:ObsIds .}
elements.sent-2411-030: The Chandra data was reprocessed with the standard pipeline in ciao version 4.15 \citep{Fruscione2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: NuSTAR data was processed with the standard pipeline in the heasoft version 6.33 tools provided by sciserver \citep{SciServer2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: For XMM, we used data products provided by the Pipeline Processing System and processed the available Observation Data Files into spectral data products using sas version 20.0.0 \citep{Gabriel2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: A primary interest of this work is the time-dependent spectral characteristics of $\gamma$\,Cas's X-ray data.
elements.sent-2411-034: Spectra were thus extracted with varying time lengths, ranging from 60\,s sub-intervals over an observation to the entire observation time.
elements.sent-2411-035: Each individual spectrum was given the same processing as appropriate for their respective observatory.
elements.sent-2411-036: Light curves are presented throughout this work.
elements.sent-2411-037: In addition to the standard count rate light curves, we utilize fluxed light curves.
elements.sent-2411-038: To generate these, we used the unfolding principle implemented in the isis software to flux-correct the counts spectra.
elements.sent-2411-039: For details on the limits and background of this method, see \citet[in prep.]{Gunderson2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-040: For a specified time binning, we extracted spectra in those intervals.
elements.sent-2411-041: These spectra were then unfolded and their flux was fit in the respective bands, generating a fluxed light curve with the same time binning as the extracted spectra.
elements.sent-2411-042: The first question we address is whether the short term variability in $\gamma$\,Cas is periodic or stochastic.
elements.sent-2411-043: In the context of the WD accretion hypothesis, either are possible based on whether the WD has a strong magnetic field or not.
elements.sent-2411-044: If the magnetic field is strong enough to control the accretion, then the accretion will be concentrated to spots on the poles of the surface that then rotate in-and-out of our view \citep{Mukai2015,Mukai2017,Lopes2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: On the other hand, if the magnetic field is too weak to direct the flows, allowing material to accrete along the equator in a boundary layer, the variability will be stochastic.
elements.sent-2411-046: The stochasticity is better described by the observed phenomena called ``flickering'' \citep{Bruch1992,Balman2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: In this situation, the change in the X-rays is caused by fluctuation in the inner edge of the accretion, though the exact mechanism is not known \citep{Bruch1992 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: An example of $\gamma$ Cas's X-ray mean-normalized light curve is given in Figure~\ref{fig:BandHRLC using its Chandra data.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Specifically, we show the light curve for the hard band (1.7 -- 7.0\,\AA, cyan) and soft band (12 -- 25\,\AA, orange).
elements.sent-2411-050: We separate these two bands to highlight the complexity of $\gamma$\,Cas's variability and that there are two different signals within the light curve.
elements.sent-2411-051: The source of the two signals will be discussed later; here we want to classify the periodicity of the composite signal of these two bands.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{gcas_band_fluxes-normed-2.pdf} \caption{Normalized light curves in the hard band ($1.7$--$7.0\,$\AA, cyan), soft band ($12$--$25\,$\AA, orange), and the hardness ratio defined as $(H-S)/(H+S)$. The $1\sigma$ boundary of the hardness ratio is shown in black dashed lines. \Change{For reference, the mean, minimum, and maximum values before normalization were $H: 110\,(72,\,146)$, $S: 18\,(8.6,\, 26)$ (both in units of $\mathrm{10^{-12}\,erg\,cm^{-2}\,s^{-1}}$), $\mathrm{HR}: 0.63\, (0.72, \,0.84).$}} \label{fig:BandHRLC}
elements.sent-2411-052: To \Change{broadly characterize the variability of $\gamma$\,Cas, we will use the autocorrelation function \begin{equation}R_{rr}(t_k) = \sum_{i=1}^n r(t_i)r(t_i-t_k),\end{equation} where $r(t_i)$ is the observed count rate in the time bin $t_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Autocorrelation is a versatile tool that can determine general correlations, periodicity, and the period of a time series data.
elements.sent-2411-054: In order to classify the periodicity of $\gamma$ Cas, we need examples of correlograms for different kinds of variability.
elements.sent-2411-055: Three stars are used: Capella, EX Hya, and SS Cyg.
elements.sent-2411-056: The Chandra HETG ObsIDs of these objects are given in Table~\ref{tab:OtherObsIds .}
elements.sent-2411-057: These observations were processed in the same pipeline as described in \S~\ref{sec:DataReduction .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The stars' correlograms are given in the left column of Figure~\ref{fig:AutoCorrPlots with 60\,s bins for all of them.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Three cases of variability are covered by our three control stars.
elements.sent-2411-060: Capella is our example of a non-variable system as it shows no variations on timescales shorter than an observation time \citep{Marshall2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: The top row of Figure~\ref{fig:AutoCorrPlots shows that there is no timescale at which the data shows significant variability.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Here ``significant'' refers to a time-lag $t_k$ whose autocorrelation value $R_{rr (t_k)$ is larger than the uncertainty (marked as red dashed lines in Figure~\ref{fig:AutoCorrPlots ) defined as $\sigma_R = 1/\sqrt{N}$, where $N$ is the number of time bins.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Next, EX Hya is an intermediate polar \citep{Mukai2023 whose 67 minute period in the X-rays has been known for decades \citep{Heise1987 .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: As such, EX Hya is an example of a periodic signal in its correlogram in the second row of Figure~\ref{fig:AutoCorrPlots .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Note that periodic signals should decay toward the end of the observation, but we have restricted all the observations to the same 20\,ks window for readability.
elements.sent-2411-066: Finally, the CV SS Cyg covers the case of a stochastic signal through its flickering \citep{Albert2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: The third row of Figure~\ref{fig:AutoCorrPlots shows that SS Cyg has many significant timescales, but there is no distinct sinusoidal pattern.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{gamma_Cas_autocorr_Chandra.pdf} \caption{Autocorrelation (left column) and partial autocorrelation (right column) of the light curves of four systems of different variabilities. Capella: no intrinsic variability (top row). Ex Hya: periodic variability (second row; period marked with the dotted blue line). SS Cyg: stochastic variability (third row). Bottom row is $\gamma$ Cas, whose variability is not a priori classified. Red dashed lines in each plot mark the significance limit for the correlogram, defined as $\sigma_R = 1/\sqrt{N}$, where $N$ is the number of time bins.} \label{fig:AutoCorrPlots}
elements.sent-2411-068: The correlogram of the Chandra observation of $\gamma$\,Cas is plotted in the last row of Figure~\ref{fig:AutoCorrPlots .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Of the three variabilities, $\gamma$\,Cas is most similar to that of SS Cyg's based on the similarity of their correlograms.
elements.sent-2411-070: However, $\gamma$\,Cas also shows more significant timescales that could be evidence of a weaker true periodicity.
elements.sent-2411-071: Regular autocorrelation makes this difficult to investigate because in a given period, $R_{rr (t_k)$ includes the stochastic signal.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The advantage of the partial autocorrelation function is that a given time $t_k$ is only compared against itself.
elements.sent-2411-073: More explicitly, the partial autocorrelation of the light curve is the autocorrelation between $r(t_i)$ and $r(t_{i+k )$ with time bins $t_{i+1}$ to $t_{i+k-1}$ removed inclusively.}
elements.sent-2411-074: As such, stochastic signals will be filtered out and reveal any periodic signals more clearly.
elements.sent-2411-075: The correlograms of the partial autocorrelation are given in the right column of Figure~\ref{fig:AutoCorrPlots .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Going down the rows, we first see again that Capella has no significant signals reinforcing the lack of true variability.
elements.sent-2411-077: EX Hya shows only significant signals around its 67 minute period (marked with a dotted blue line).
elements.sent-2411-078: Moving to SS Cyg, we see that for a stochastic signal only the first immediate time bin is significant.\footnote{By ``first time bin'' we mean the first after $t=0$\,s, so $t=60$\,s in our case.}
elements.sent-2411-079: By definition, the autocorrelation and partial autocorrelation functions are equal to 1 at $t=0$\,s.
elements.sent-2411-080: This can be interpreted as only adjacent time bins, i.e. those only 60\,s apart based on our binning, are correlated with each other.
elements.sent-2411-081: Finally, comparing these to $\gamma$ Cas, we see that only the first non-zero time bin is significant, just as in SS\,Cyg.
elements.sent-2411-082: We can conclude that there is no periodic signal in $\gamma$\,Cas, i.e, both the soft and hard wavebands vary stochastically.
elements.sent-2411-083: Returning to the the light curves in Figure~\ref{fig:BandHRLC , we can note that the variations in the two bands are sometimes correlated but at other times uncorrelated.}
elements.sent-2411-084: These were noted by \citet{Smith2019 and further analyzed by \citet{Rauw2022 , who attributed it to changes in emission and absorption from ``canopies" of gas in their magnetic shearing model.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: However, we argue that the variability in both hard and soft bands is indicative of not just changes in absorption (which will primarily affect the softer wavelengths) but also two different emission sources.
elements.sent-2411-086: In Figure~\ref{fig:BandHRLC , we also include the mean normalized hardness ratio \begin{equation}\mathrm{HR} = \frac{H-S}{H+S},\end{equation} where $H$ and $S$ are the hard ($1.7$--$7.0\,$\AA) and soft ($12$--$25\,$\AA) flux values respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The HR is driven by the changes in the soft flux, as was noted in prior works, but what we want to highlight is the quasi-independence of the hard flux to this absorption.
elements.sent-2411-088: In the top panel of Figure~\ref{fig:SpecRatioHR we show the HEG+MEG HETG spectra (on the MEG grid) of $\gamma$\,Cas in high (red) and low (blue) hardness states.}
elements.sent-2411-089: We define these hardness states as the times when $\mathrm{HR}\gtrless\overline{\mathrm{HR }\pm\sigma_\mathrm{HR}$, where $\overline{\mathrm{HR }$ is the mean HR and $\sigma_\mathrm{HR}$ is the standard deviation.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: These two plots show the primary effect is absorption as the high HR state has less flux in the longer wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-091: This is shown more explicitly in the bottom plot where the ratio of the high HR to low HR in every wavelength bin is plotted.
elements.sent-2411-092: Two absorption models plotted: simple slab absorption (``PHABS,'' cyan) and partial covering (``PCFABS,'' orange).
elements.sent-2411-093: Both these models use an increase in column density of $N_\mathrm{H =0.2\times10^{22}\,\mathrm{cm^{-2 }$, assuming solar abundances \citep{Anders1989 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Despite the increase in column density, the ($\lambda<7$\,\AA) are unaffected by the absorption.
elements.sent-2411-095: Yet in Figure~\ref{fig:BandHRLC the hard band is varying just as much as the soft band.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Hence we argue that there is a second, true source variability in $\gamma$\,Cas associated with its X-ray generation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-097: This is more easily seen in Figure~\ref{fig:HRvsFlux where we plot the HR against the soft and hard flux values in every bin.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The correlation between HR and soft flux is plainly visible in the linear trend with the soft flux (red points).
elements.sent-2411-099: The hard flux (black points) on the other hand show no correlation.
elements.sent-2411-100: Instead, there is a random distribution of the hard flux over all HR.
elements.sent-2411-101: We argue that we are seeing true source variability in this case.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{gcas_flux_ratio_vs_HR-01.pdf} \caption{Top panel: spectra filtered by hardness ratio ranges. The ``Low Hardness'' spectrum (blue) is from times when the HR was at least $1\sigma$ below the mean hardness ratio, and the ``High Hardness'' from time when HR was at least $\sigma$ above the mean. Bottom: the ratio of the high to low HR spectra (black points with error bars, for points with $>2\sigma$ significance). The colored curves show absorption functions for slab absorption (``PHABS,'' cyan) and partial covering (``PCFABS,'' orange) for an increase in the column by $0.2\times10^{22}\,\mathrm{cm^{-2}}$ from the nominal models, showing that the higher hardness state is consistent with increased absorption.} \label{fig:SpecRatioHR}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{gcas_HR_vs_hs_flux.pdf} \caption{Hardness ratios against band flux. Points are shown twice, once against the soft flux ($12$--$25\,$\AA, left side, red), and again vs hard flux ($1.7$--$7.0\,$\AA, right side, black). Note the strong correlation of the hardness ratio with soft flux, and lack of correlation with hard flux.} \label{fig:HRvsFlux}
elements.sent-2411-102: Given we are assuming the X-ray emission source is an accreting WD, this source variability is likely the flickering like in SS Cyg.
elements.sent-2411-103: More importantly, since there is no periodicity in the signals, we can argue against the \Change{intermediate polar accretion mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Thus we can conclude that an accretion disk is present and extends down to the surface, creating a boundary layer where the accretion X-rays are generated.
elements.sent-2411-105: The XMM portion was used by \citet{Smith2019 for detailed investigation of the timing data.}
elements.sent-2411-106: The NuSTAR half was used by \citet{Tsujimoto2018 alongside a Suzaku observation taken on 2011-07-13 to investigate the WD accretion hypothesis.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The inclusion of the Suzaku data was to constrain the low energy portion of $\gamma$\,Cas's emission, but we argue that observations taken at different times cannot be fit simultaneously because of $\gamma$\,Cas's variability between observation epochs \citep{Rauw2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-108: By using the simultaneous XMM and NuSTAR data together, our model fitting is not subject to the epochal variations and will better describe the snapshot of the system.
elements.sent-2411-109: We will analyze the temporal variations in the spectra later in this section, but we first need to know the baseline, average properties of the spectra.
elements.sent-2411-110: For this, we will fit XMM's Reflection Grating Spectrometer (RGS) and NuSTAR's Focal Plane Modules A and B (FPMA/B).
elements.sent-2411-111: While the XMM's EPIC pn (hereafter PN) or Metal Oxide Semi-conductor (MOS) cameras would provide additional counts in the high energy portion where NuSTAR is sensitive, the high energy portion of the spectra is primarily continuum emission (see Figures~\ref{fig:RGSNuStargCas or \ref{fig:DynamicSpectra for example) beyond the Fe~K\,$\alpha$ complex.}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Additional photons would not significantly improve the signal-to-noise given the added computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-113: The emission lines in the RGS spectra, on the other hand, provide better constraints on the differential emission measure (DEM) distribution while also adding the low energy spectrum we need.
elements.sent-2411-114: When fitting the spectra of $\gamma$\,Cas and its analogues, it is common to use multi-temperature plasma models so as to fit both the H-like and He-like ions.
elements.sent-2411-115: H-like ion emissivity curves have long, high temperature tails that allow them to emit at much higher temperatures that are too hot for He-like emission (see figure 3 of \citealp{Gunderson2023 for an example of emissivity curves).}
elements.sent-2411-116: However, their interpretation of the different temperature components was not of two different sources.
elements.sent-2411-117: In comparison, \citet{Hamaguchi2016 made the first direct interpretation of two emission sources based on Suzaku data, specifically inferring polar accretion to be the source of the hot component and wind interactions between the Be star and WD as the soft source(s).}
elements.sent-2411-118: Our goal is to build off these multi-temperature, multi-source models to create a complete picture of the X-ray generation mechanism in $\gamma$\,Cas.
elements.sent-2411-119: Specifically, one model will be used to describe the WD accretion whereas the second source will be an unknown component that we aim to elucidate possible information on.
elements.sent-2411-120: As will be shown below, this second source is possibly the interaction between the WD's accretion disk and the surrounding Be decretion disk.
elements.sent-2411-121: The first component is a spherically symmetric cooling flow to describe the accretion of material onto the WD, as was used by \citet{Tsujimoto2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-122: The luminosity of the cooling flow is \begin{equation}L(\lambda)d\lambda = \frac{5}{2}\frac{k_B\dot{M}}{\mu m_p}\int_{T_\mathrm{min}}^{T_\mathrm{max,1}}\frac{\Lambda_\lambda(T)}{\Lambda_\mathrm{Bolo}(T)}\left(\frac{T}{T_\mathrm{max,1}}\right)^\beta dTd\lambda,\end{equation} where $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, $\mu=0.67$ is the mean molecular weight, $m_p$ is the proton mass, $\dot{M}$ is the WD accretion rate, $T_\mathrm{min}$ and $T_\mathrm{max,1}$ are the lower and upper limits of the cooling flow's temperature distribution, $\Lambda_\lambda(T)$ is the emissivity of the plasma at the wavelength $\lambda$, and $\Lambda_\mathrm{bolo (T)$ is the bolometric emissivity \citep{Fabian1994 .}}
elements.sent-2411-123: This component is accompanied by a partial covering fraction absorption model \begin{equation}M_\mathrm{pcfabs}(\lambda) = f \mathrm{e}^{-n_{\mathrm{H},1} \sigma(\lambda)}+(1-f),\end{equation} where $f$ is the fraction of the boundary layer that is covered.
elements.sent-2411-124: The free parameters of the cooling flow are the maximum temperature $T_\mathrm{max,1}$, mass-accretion rate $\dot{M}$, and the power law slope $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-125: The minimum temperature $T_\mathrm{min}$ was frozen at $T_\mathrm{min =1$\,MK.}
elements.sent-2411-126: When both the minimum and maximum temperatures were allowed to be free, we found that the model fitting became highly degenerate.
elements.sent-2411-127: When one parameter would be constrained, the other would not be.
elements.sent-2411-128: Freezing the minimum temperature to its lower bound thus served to constrain the model parameter fitting.
elements.sent-2411-129: We also included a Gaussian line model centered on the Fe~K\,$\alpha$ line (6.4\,keV) with an assumed, frozen width of $\sigma_{\mathrm{K}\,\alpha = 0.05$\,keV.}
elements.sent-2411-130: There are physically important processes that the Fe~K\,$\alpha$'s presence signifies \citep{Mukai2015,Mukai2017,Hayashi2018 , but these processes are not within the scope of this work.}
elements.sent-2411-131: As such, the flux in the Fe~K\,$\alpha$ is a free parameter for improving the fit statistic and better constraining the other parameters in our model.
elements.sent-2411-132: The second model component we include is for the unknown second source in the system.
elements.sent-2411-133: While multi-temperature plasma models, i.e., multi-temperature apec models, can adequately fit the data, they introduce many more parameters.
elements.sent-2411-134: To prevent our parameter space from growing too large, we used a power law DEM \begin{equation}\frac{d\mathrm{EM}}{dT} = D_0(d)\left(\frac{T}{T_\mathrm{max,2}}\right)^\gamma,\end{equation} where $T_\mathrm{max,2}$ is the maximum temperature of the second source, $\gamma$ is the slope of the distribution, and $D_0(d)$ is a distance $d$ dependent normalization, to describe a multi-temperature source.
elements.sent-2411-135: This power law DEM is constructed through a weighted sum of isothermal plasma models; for more details on the model, see \citet{Huenemoerder2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-136: This component will have its own absorption model applied to it, specifically a simple slab absorption \begin{equation}M_\mathrm{phabs}(\lambda) = \mathrm{e}^{-n_\mathrm{H,2}\sigma(\lambda)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-137: In our modelling, we allowed the two column densities $n_\mathrm{H,1}$ and $n_\mathrm{H,2}$ to be independent.
elements.sent-2411-138: This is again a choice based on our lack of knowledge of the secondary source.
elements.sent-2411-139: It may be the case that the two sources are geometrically related such that they share the same absorption, but those are details we aim to find.
elements.sent-2411-140: The first step in our model fitting was a simple model fit to find the appropriate line broadening for the two models to improve the fit statistic.
elements.sent-2411-141: These came out to $v_1 = 697$\,km\,s$^{-1}$ for the cooling flow model and $v_2=661$\,km\,s$^{-1}$ for the power law DEM.
elements.sent-2411-142: These velocities were frozen in our full Markov Chain Monte Carlo model fits.
elements.sent-2411-143: The final parameters of our model are the abundances of different elements.
elements.sent-2411-144: Elements whose emission lines are resolved in the RGS spectra had freely fit abundances.
elements.sent-2411-145: Those that were not detected in the RGS had their abundances frozen at unity, relative to \citet{Anders1989 .}
elements.sent-2411-146: Both of our model components are plasma models with their own abundance parameters.
elements.sent-2411-147: We set these parameters equal to each other so that we have only one set of abundance parameters describing the X-ray emitting plasma.
elements.sent-2411-148: This is not necessarily a requirement and could be relaxed to have two independent plasma models, like the absorption components.
elements.sent-2411-149: However, whatever the second source is, if it is within the $\gamma$\,Cas system, it should have a similar chemical composition.
elements.sent-2411-150: The final modelling choice of note is how we applied the model to the data.
elements.sent-2411-151: Our assumption has been that the second source is only seen in the softer portion of the spectra, so it should not provide any flux to the harder portion.
elements.sent-2411-152: To account for this, we applied the power law DEM to only the RGS.
elements.sent-2411-153: The RGS model was then the sum of the power law DEM and the cooling flow with their respective absorption components.
elements.sent-2411-154: The FPMA/B spectra only had the cooling flow applied to them along with an overall cross-calibration constants.
elements.sent-2411-155: The RGS is well-calibrated against the PN camera\footnote{\url{https://xmmweb.esac.esa.int/docs/documents/CAL-TN-0052.pdf to agree up to $\mathrm{RGS /\mathrm{PN = 0.95$ whereas \citet{Madsen2017 gives the PN camera to be in agreement with the FPMA up to $\mathrm{PN /\mathrm{FPMA =0.88-0.90$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: We propagated these cross-calibration constants to determine that the RGS-to-NuSTAR constant should be $\mathrm{RGS /\mathrm{FPMA = 0.84$.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: However, we found that this value did not match the simultaneous data.
elements.sent-2411-158: As such, we allowed a cross-calibration constant to be free during initial fits, finding $\mathrm{RGS /\mathrm{FPMA = 0.70$.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: We used the S-Lang emcee package in isis to fit our model using Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods.

elements.sent-2411-169: A common argument against the accreting WD hypothesis is that there is not enough mass within the Be decretion disk to support the calculated accretion rates.
elements.sent-2411-170: As a test, we can estimate the amount of accretion that the disk can support from a massive body moving through it using Bondi-Hoyle accretion \begin{equation}\dot{M}_\mathrm{BH}\approx \pi\frac{\rho G^2 M^2}{c_s^3},\end{equation} where $G$ is the gravitional constant, $\rho$ is the density of the accreting material, $M$ is the WD mass, and $c_s$ is the sound speed of the gas around the accreting body.
elements.sent-2411-171: First, we need a mass of the WD.
elements.sent-2411-172: We can get this from the max temperature of the cooling flow.
elements.sent-2411-173: In a non-magnetic CV, only half the gravitational potential energy is converted to kinetic energy \citep{Ezuka1999,Yu2018 , so the maximum temperature is \begin{equation}k_B T_\mathrm{max,1} = 8\left(\frac{M}{0.5 M_\odot}\right)\left(\frac{R_\mathrm{WD}}{10^9 \mathrm{cm}}\right)^{-1}\,\mathrm{keV} \label{eq:kTtoM}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Combining this with the mass-radius relation in for WDs \begin{equation}R_\mathrm{WD} = 0.78\times10^9\left(\left(\frac{1.44 M_\odot}{M_\mathrm{WD}}\right)^{2/3} - \left(\frac{M_\mathrm{WD}}{1.44 M_\odot}\right)^{2/3}\right)^{1/2}\,\mathrm{cm},\label{eq:MassRadiusWD}\end{equation} \citep{Ezuka1999 , we can estimate the WD mass to be $M_\mathrm{WD =0.93\,M_\odot$.}}
elements.sent-2411-175: Other relevant parameters of the $\gamma$\,Cas system are given in Table~\ref{tab:WDProps , including quantities found in the literature and calculated here.}
elements.sent-2411-176: During subsequent observations, the overall flickering amplitude of the system may be higher or lower, changing the average spectra and subsequently the model parameters, such as those found by \citet{Tsujimoto2018 when using non-simultaneous data.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Next we need the density of the decretion disk material at the radius at which the WD orbits.
elements.sent-2411-178: The density of Be decretion disks in the viscous limit \citep{Klement2017 can be modeled by \begin{equation}\rho_\mathrm{DD}(r,z) = \rho_0 \left(\frac{r}{R_*}\right)^{-\alpha} \mathrm{exp}\left(-\frac{z^2}{2H^2}\right),\label{eq:BeDiskDensity}\end{equation} where $\rho_0$ is the central density of the disk near the surface of the Be star, $r$ is the disk radius, $R_*$ is the radius of the Be star, $z$ is the height above or below the disk.}
elements.sent-2411-179: The scale height of the disk $H$ is also dependent on the radius from the star: \begin{equation}H(r)=H_0 \left(\frac{r}{R_*}\right)^{3/2},\label{eq:ScaleHeight}\end{equation} where $H_0 = c_s R_*/v_\mathrm{orb}$ is the scale height at the base of the disk and $v_\mathrm{orb}$ is the orbital velocity of the disk material.
elements.sent-2411-180: The parameter $\alpha$ describes how fast the density drops in the disk, which varies from star to star.
elements.sent-2411-181: In steady-state, the exponent is $\alpha=3.5$ \citep{Klement2017 , which we adopt here.}
elements.sent-2411-182: The radius of interest corresponds to the WD's semi-major axis $a_\mathrm{WD}$, which we can get from the ephemeris from \citet{Nemravova2012 and our calculated WD mass, all of which are given in Table~\ref{tab:WDProps .}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Note though that this will not be the radius within the decretion disk, which is measured from the Be star.
elements.sent-2411-184: Since the WD's mass is not neglible, the system's center mass is $a_* = (M_\mathrm{WD /M_*)a_\mathrm{WD = 1.93_{-0.02 ^{+0.08}\,R_*$ from the Be star, so the radius within the Be decretion disk is $r=a_\mathrm{WD +a_*$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Assuming that the WD orbits within the $z=0$ central portion of the decretion disk, the density at $r=a_\mathrm{WD +a_*$ is $\log\rho\approx-15.99_{-0.05 ^{+0.08}$\,dex\,g\,cm$^{-3}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Finally, the sound speed of the gas $c_s = \sqrt{5 k_BT/3 \mu m_p}$ around this point is dependent on the temperature of the disk.

elements.sent-2411-279: Be decretion disks are known to have complex structures, such as spiral arm density waves or radially flowing clumps \citep{Okazaki2002,Escolano2015,Cyr2020,Suffak2022 .}

elements.sent-2411-312: We showed that the X-ray variability in $\gamma$\,Cas shows different signatures between the hard and soft bands.
elements.sent-2411-313: The latter appears to be purely due to absorption, whereas the former shows the same autocorrelation as flickering in non-magnetic CVs.
elements.sent-2411-314: We also analyzed the spectra from simultaneous XMM and NuSTAR observations within the framework of two different emission sources based on both the timing analysis and similar, albeit less directly interpreted, modeling in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-315: Our model consisted of a cooling flow model to describe the hypothesized WD that is generating the hard X-rays along with a power-law DEM for the unknown, softer secondary source.
elements.sent-2411-316: The best-fit parameters on the cooling flow model were consistent with estimates of the WD properties from optical studies, adding further evidence for the WD hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-317: The power law DEM provided reasonably constrained parameters and was shown to reproduce the He-like lines in the RGS that the cooling flow could not.
elements.sent-2411-318: This second source is proposed to be a shock between the in-falling Be decretion disk material trapped by the WD's gravitational well and the WD's accretion disk.
elements.sent-2411-319: The shock is specifically due to the difference in angular momentum between the in falling gas and the disk, producing characteristically softer X-rays than the accretion shock at the base of the accretion disk.
elements.sent-2411-320: Our estimate of the maximum temperature of this shock $T_\mathrm{max,2}$ was the only under-constrained parameter due to the large positive error.

elements.sent-2411-328: Finally, we analyzed a unique feature of the XMM dataset, which we call a deep absorption event, that shows a remarkable loss of half the expected count rate over an extended period of almost 2\,ks.
elements.sent-2411-329: The exact cause of the \Change{deep absorption event is not known, but we argue that it is evidence of the WD encountering some form of decretion disk instability/structure that enveloped the WD and its \Change{accretion disk and significantly increased the absorption.}}
elements.sent-2411-330: Afterwards, there is a commensurate rise in the count rate, possibly due to extra material entering the accretion system and pushing on the \Change{accretion disk .}
elements.sent-2411-331: A question of interest is the frequency of \Change{deep absorption events .}
elements.sent-2411-332: If the \Change{deep absorption event was caused by a spiral arm in the Be disk, there will be a periodicity like the interactions of the neutron star and gas stream in GX\,301-2 \citep{Leahy2008 .}}
elements.sent-2411-333: One-arm spiral arm waves have previously been detected in $\gamma$\,Cas \citep{Berio1999 , but subsequent measurements found that this arm disappeared \citep{Stee2012 .}}
elements.sent-2411-334: On the other hand, if the WD encountered a clump in the disk, it will be a stochastic event with no periodicity.
elements.sent-2411-335: With only one example of a \Change{deep absorption event , we cannot make any statements of periods.}
elements.sent-2411-336: There are potentially more of these events in Figure~\ref{fig:PNMovingAverage , such as between 15 -- 20 ks, but the enhanced flickering makes it difficult to pinpoint the U-shape characteristic of the \Change{deep absorption event .}}
elements.sent-2411-337: NICER has observed $\gamma$\,Cas across approximately 2 consecutive orbital periods.
elements.sent-2411-338: As a timing-focused instrument sensitive to softer X-rays, NICER is well equipped to answer the question of \Change{deep absorption event frequencies.}
elements.sent-2411-339: If a \Change{deep absorption event is found at the same orbital phase, then it may be evidence of a new one-arm wave in the Be disk.}
elements.sent-2411-340: This work shows that the WD hypothesis for $\gamma$\,Cas's X-ray emission can explain both spectral and temporal features.
elements.sent-2411-341: Yet simple accretion X-ray generation is not enough to fully explain what we see, adding further complications and mysteries to untangle.
